retrieved_docs.title: Care of a Chest Tube -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumothorax Spontaneous pneumothorax Occurs without trauma, often in individuals with underlying lung conditions or in tall, thin individuals. Chest tube drainage remains the reference first-line treatment of primary spontaneous pneumothorax. [2] Tension pneumothorax This is a medical emergency caused by progressive accumulation of air in the pleural space, leading to lung collapse, mediastinal shift, impaired venous return, and cardiopulmonary compromise. Traumatic or iatrogenic pneumothorax This results from penetrating or blunt trauma or secondary to diagnostic and therapeutic pulmonary procedures. [3] New guidelines on primary spontaneous pneumothorax suggest ambulatory approaches may be suitable. However, guidance on iatrogenic pneumothorax occurring in patients with impaired lung function, increased age, comorbidity, and frailty is lacking, and the safety profile of ambulatory management is not known. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Patient Selection -- Issues of Concern -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Recent thoracic surgery (recommend imaging to rule out pneumothorax)

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Oxygen Therapy Contraindications -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Emergency medical personnel are responsible for obtaining the initial history, which may reveal indications for emergent HBOT, such as diving injuries or carbon monoxide toxicity. Radiology staff must review prior imaging for abnormalities, such as pulmonary nodules, that may contraindicate elective HBOT. In emergent situations, radiologists are vital in ruling out tension pneumothorax, the only absolute contraindication to HBOT.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperbaric Patient Selection -- Issues of Concern -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The one absolute contraindication to hyperbaric oxygen treatment is a patient with an untreated pneumothorax. All patients should have lung imaging before treatment. If a patient receives pneumothorax treatment, the risk-benefit ratio would need evaluation before placing the patient in the chamber based on their indication. During pneumothorax management, a chest tube with the Heimlich valve open before initiation of treatment would be an acceptable approach for pre-chamber management in an emergency clinical situation. Relative contraindications to evaluate before treatment include, but are not limited to, the following:

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Pneumothorax -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical signs and symptoms suggestive of primary and secondary spontaneous pneumothorax. Implement guideline-based management strategies tailored to the severity and type of pneumothorax. Assess radiographic findings to confirm diagnosis and monitor progression or resolution of pneumothorax. Collaborate effectively with interprofessional team members—such as radiologists, pulmonologists, emergency clinicians, nurses, and thoracic surgeons—to coordinate timely diagnosis and optimize treatment outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Pneumothorax -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: There are no absolute contraindications for treatment of tension pneumothorax with needle thoracostomy or treatment of communicating pneumothorax with an occlusive dressing. The overall condition of the patient, time to transport to a facility, and other patient-specific factors such as chronic anticoagulation can help the practitioner make the decision of whether to pursue more invasive measures or delay therapy to more definitive treatment such as thoracostomy in the hospital setting. Ultimately, given the high morbidity and mortality associated with these injuries, the treatment is often necessary and should not be delayed in the unstable patient for any of these factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pneumothorax Evaluation and Treatment -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the clinical signs and symptoms of pneumothorax. Assess imaging studies, such as chest x-rays and ultrasound, for accurate diagnosis and monitoring of pneumothorax progression. Apply evidence-based guidelines to determine when conservative management or surgical intervention is necessary. Collaborate with emergency physicians, thoracic surgeons, pulmonologists, radiologists, nurses, and respiratory therapists to optimize patient outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Tension Pneumothorax -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: The following are the key points in managing tension pneumothorax: Tension pneumothorax is a clinical diagnosis. The condition may arise from traumatic and atraumatic causes and prehospital and in-hospital settings. If a patient is hemodynamically unstable and a tension pneumothorax is likely, needle decompression must be performed immediately, followed by CTT. If the patient is stable, diagnostic imaging such as a chest x-ray may be obtained before treatment. Patients with pulmonary conditions predisposing them to high peak inspiratory pressures are at greater risk of tension pneumothorax. CTT suffices for managing most pneumothorax cases. Further surgical treatments, including VATS with pleurodesis, may be necessary in others. Quick diagnosis and management can prevent significant morbidity and mortality from this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Care of a Chest Tube -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Severe pulmonary disease Conditions like advanced pulmonary fibrosis or bullous emphysema may increase the risk of complications, such as lung puncture or air leaks.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Pneumothorax Evaluation and Treatment -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Tension pneumothorax is a medical emergency and should be treated with immediate needle decompression followed by chest tube placement.

retrieved_docs.title: Spontaneous Pneumothorax -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This educational activity provides clinicians with updated guidance on diagnosing and managing spontaneous pneumothorax, including insights from recent British Thoracic Society and European Respiratory Society guidelines. Participants enhance their ability to select appropriate imaging modalities, differentiate between PSP and SSP, and determine the most effective treatment pathways based on patient-specific risk factors and presentation. Emphasis is placed on decision-making frameworks that favor conservative management when clinically appropriate, while recognizing scenarios that warrant escalation to surgical intervention. Collaboration with an interprofessional team—including emergency clinicians, pulmonologists, radiologists, thoracic surgeons, and nurses—ensures coordinated, evidence-based care that reduces complications and recurrence rates while optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Iatrogenic Pneumothorax -- Treatment / Management -- Management Algorithm for Iatrogenic Pneumothorax
retrieved_docs.content: Definitive treatment options: Needle aspiration Chest tube drainage VATS Open surgical intervention

retrieved_docs.title: Care of a Chest Tube -- Contraindications -- Relative Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Clinically insignificant pneumothorax or effusion In asymptomatic individuals, observation or less invasive management (eg, needle aspiration) may be preferable to chest tube placement.

retrieved_docs.title: Chest Trauma -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Injury-specific interventions are required following the primary survey, prioritizing airway, breathing, and circulation stabilization. [29] Prompt interventions, such as emergent chest tube thoracostomy (CTT), are necessary for managing immediate life-threatening injuries, including large pneumothoraces and hemothoraces. Patients with pneumothorax should receive 100% oxygen via a nonrebreather mask, with needle decompression followed by CTT if tension pneumothorax is suspected.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Pneumothorax -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Pneumothorax should be considered in the setting of thoracic trauma where there are clinical signs of respiratory or circulatory compromise. Classically the findings of hypoxia, chest wall crepitus, diminished breath sounds, tachypnea, tachycardia, hypotension, tracheal deviation, and the presence of a sucking chest wound all have been used to help make the diagnosis. Many of these findings are observed in the primary survey of the trauma patient and can be obtained by the prehospital provider. It is paramount to note that some of these findings, which are used in protocolized guidelines of treatment, are somewhat unreliable and may only be late findings. [5] [6] [7] [8] [9]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 862
retrieved_docs.content: دبيلة is associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, indicating that individuals with دبيلة may develop these respiratory complications. Recognizing this relationship is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and appropriate management of patients presenting with دبيلة.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1001
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of the thorax. This subdivision of the thorax is directly linked to the thoracic conditions pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. The three pathologies—pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse—are also associated with the term “دبيلة”. Recognizing that closed drainage of chest is employed to treat pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse underscores its importance in thoracic management and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2137
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest has a relationship that it is a subdivision of thorax, indicating that this closed‑system chest tube technique is classified within thoracic procedures. As a subdivision of thorax, closed drainage of chest is employed to evacuate air, fluid, or blood from the pleural space in conditions such as pneumothorax, hemothorax, or pleural effusion, and its categorization guides clinicians in selecting appropriate thoracic interventions and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: group. The most precise form, chest‑tube placement (تركيب أنبوب الصدر), is a narrow subset of this procedural hierarchy. Overall, the framework delineates how imaging, diagnostic aspiration, and a spectrum of chest‑tube and shunt procedures are anatomically anchored to the chest and pleural spaces and interrelated with pleural disorders such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, pulmonary collapse, and pleural neoplasms, thereby guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate diagnostic and therapeutic strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 3 -- Community 2184
retrieved_docs.content: The term “hrudník,” referring to the chest, denotes the anatomical location where pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse can develop. These three thoracic conditions are also linked with “دبيلة,” indicating an association between the clinical manifestations of pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse and the entity دبيلة. Understanding that the chest (hrudník) is the site of these pathologies and recognizing their connection to دبيلة is important for accurate diagnosis and effective management of patients presenting with pleural and pulmonary disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
retrieved_docs.content: The subdivision of thorax, identified in Arabic as دبيلة, corresponds to the pleural membrane and is directly linked to pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. These three conditions all involve the pleura: pleurisy denotes inflammation of the pleural layers, pneumothorax refers to the presence of air within the pleural space that disrupts normal lung expansion, and pulmonary collapse (atelectasis) often results from pleural pathology that prevents the lung from remaining inflated. Recognizing that the subdivision of thorax (دبيلة) is the anatomical structure underlying these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic intervention, and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 194
retrieved_docs.content: the parent procedure “incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage.” Narrower techniques include pleural cavity aspiration (رشف التجويف الجنبي), insertion of an underwater‑seal chest drain, tube thoracostomy with a water seal, chest‑tube placement (تركيب أنبوب الصدر), closed drainage of the chest, and creation of a pleuroperitoneal shunt (which also involves incision of the chest wall and pleura). These procedures target pleural pathologies (abscess, hemothorax, empyema) and mediastinal complications, providing coordinated diagnostic and therapeutic pathways for comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 865
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure described as incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage includes the specific technique رشف التجويف الجنبي, indicating that pleural cavity aspiration is a component of the broader chest drainage and thoracentesis interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 813
retrieved_docs.content: Internal injury of chest is recognized as a traumatic event and functions as a cause of subsequent medical complications, highlighting its role as an initiating injury in clinical assessments and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 186
retrieved_docs.content: Closed chest drainage is classified within the thoracic subdivision of procedures that involve incision of the pleura, thoracentesis, and chest drainage. This subdivision is directly linked to pleural‑cavity pathologies such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, which are considered manifestations of a broader side‑related disorder (اضطراب جنبي) and are also associated with the term دبيلة. Interventional procedures targeting the pleural space include creation of a pleuroperitoneal shunt, side‑cavity aspiration (رشف التجويف الجنبي), insertion of an underwater‑seal chest drain, and tube thoracostomy with a water seal for indications such as abscess, hemothorax, and empyema. Tube thoracostomy and chest tube insertion (تركيب أنبوب الصدر) are narrower procedures that fall under the overarching category of incision of pleura; thoracentesis; chest drainage, with the latter three actions (incision of pleura, thoracentesis, chest drainage) being directly related to chest‑tube placement. Pleural neoplasms of uncertain or unknown behavior are also linked to incision of the chest wall and pleura within this hierarchy. Understanding the network of thoracic anatomical subdivisions, pleural diseases, and their associated diagnostic and therapeutic interventions is essential for effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1344
retrieved_docs.content: Timolol, a non‑selective beta‑adrenergic blocker, should not be used in patients who have تَشَنُّج قَصَبِيّ (bronchial spasm or asthma) because the drug can worsen airway constriction and precipitate respiratory distress. Recognizing this contraindication is essential for safe prescribing and for preventing adverse respiratory events in individuals with underlying bronchospastic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 4 -- Community 2792
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a specific type of lung disorder (اِضطِراب الرِّئَة), indicating that it falls under the broader category of pulmonary disturbances. Recognizing this hierarchical relationship helps clinicians understand that respiratory distress syndrome shares fundamental characteristics with other lung disorders, which can guide accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment strategies, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 2 -- Community 974
retrieved_docs.content: Closed drainage of chest is an indirect procedure performed at the anatomical region referred to as hrudník (the chest). The hrudník serves as the location of several thoracic pathologies, including pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure is also linked to the Subdivision of thorax, a categorical segment of the thoracic cavity that itself has a relationship with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. Together, these connections underscore that closed chest drainage is specifically directed toward the chest area and the thoracic subdivision that are commonly affected by pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse, highlighting the procedural relevance to these conditions in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.
